title: Draught-sealing around roof window frames
subtitle: Closing the air gap between a Velux-type roof window frame and the surrounding loft insulation
excerpt: Roof windows are typically installed with insulation pulled back 50 to 100 mm from the frame to prevent moisture accumulation. Over years, that gap becomes a convection pathway around the window reveal. Sealing the gap with mineral wool collars and vapour-control tape eliminates the draught path without creating condensation risk.
sourceNotes: Velux installation guides on insulation clearance requirements. gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B on roof window insulation detailing. Energy Saving Trust guidance on roof insulation continuity.

glossaryTerms.definition: A low-permeability membrane fitted on the warm side of insulation to slow vapour diffusion and reduce the risk of condensation forming within the construction.

content.text: Roof window frames are typically installed with a 50 to 100 mm gap in the surrounding loft insulation to prevent moisture building up at the frame flange. Over time this gap becomes a convection pathway. Cold loft air circulates around the frame, chilling the reveal and adding 20 to 30 percent to heat loss through the window. Fitting a loose mineral wool collar and taping the
to the frame flange closes this pathway without blocking the ventilation gap that prevents frame rot.
Flexible mineral wool quilt (90 mm or 100 mm thick) to fill the gap around the frame as a collar
Airtightness tape, 50 mm wide, to seal the vapour control membrane to the frame flange
A piece of vapour control membrane if the existing loft membrane does not reach the frame
In the loft, pull back the existing insulation from around the window to expose the frame flange and the gap between frame and insulation.

Check the frame flange for signs of moisture: rust-coloured staining, paint flaking, or soft timber. If moisture is present, identify and fix the source (typically a failed flashing or a drip from condensation on the frame) before sealing.

Cut strips of flexible mineral wool to fill the gap between the frame and the surrounding insulation. Fit them as a snug collar, not compressed but in firm contact with the frame flange and the existing insulation edge. The collar should be the same depth as the existing insulation layer.

If the loft has a vapour control membrane installed, tape it to the frame flange with airtightness tape, forming a continuous layer that closes the warm-air pathway from the room below into the insulation collar. Overlap the tape 50 mm onto both the membrane and the frame flange.

Reinstate the surrounding insulation to its original depth. Do not compress the collar against the frame. The insulation must be able to settle without pushing against the frame and causing moisture accumulation at the timber.

attrs.symptom: Condensation appears on the frame after sealing

content.text: The collar has blocked ventilation at the frame edge. Remove 20 mm of collar from the immediate frame edge to restore a small air gap, then seal only the membrane without filling the full gap with insulation at the frame line.

attrs.symptom: Cold air still felt around the window reveal

content.text: The draught is coming from below, through the ceiling around the window reveal in the room below, not from the loft gap. Seal the ceiling junction inside the room with a fine bead of decorators caulk around the reveal trim.
